---
title: "Adding an AI Chat Assistant to a Static Website"
author:
  - name: Guillaume Gilles
  - name: Claude Sonnet 4.6
    url: https://www.anthropic.com/claude/sonnet
    roles:
      - reviewing code
      - writing - review & editing
abstract: |
  Most personal websites are one-way streets: you publish, visitors read.
  What if they could *ask* instead? In this post I walk through adding a
  small AI assistant — powered by OpenAI and Cloudflare Workers — that lets
  anyone query my experience and résumé in plain English, with zero backend
  server to maintain.
date: 2026-03-08
date-modified: 2026-04-07
image: image.png
categories: [AI, Cloudflare, Quarto, Web]
page-layout: full
execute:
  echo: false

filters:
  - ../../_extensions/qmd-lab/closeread/closeread.lua
format:
  html:
    css: ../../_extensions/qmd-lab/closeread/closeread.css
---

## Why bother?

A PDF résumé is a frozen snapshot. Visitors have to skim it, guess what is
relevant to them, and hope they find the right section. A recruiter looking
for Python experience and a professor curious about your teaching history
need very different things — but they both have to read the same document.

An AI assistant changes that dynamic. Instead of making the visitor do the
work, you let them ask in plain language: *"Does he have any experience with
time-series forecasting?"* or *"What level does he teach at?"* and get a
direct, personalised answer in seconds.

The goal of this post is to build exactly that, in a way that is:

- **Free (or nearly so)** — Cloudflare Workers has a generous free tier (100,000 requests/day); OpenAI costs fractions of a cent per conversation.
- **Private** — your OpenAI key never touches the browser; only your Worker ever sees it.
- **Maintenance-free** — no server to patch, no database, no uptime to worry about.
- **Portable** — the approach works with any static site generator (Quarto, Hugo, Jekyll, plain HTML…).

## The big picture

Three moving parts, one simple flow:

```
Visitor's browser
      │  1. types a question
      ▼
Chat widget (HTML/JS)
      │  2. POST { message, history } to Worker URL
      ▼
Cloudflare Worker  ──►  OpenAI API
      │                     │
      │  3. system prompt   │  4. returns answer
      │     + message       │
      ◄─────────────────────┘
      │  5. forwards { reply }
      ▼
Chat widget displays reply
```

**The widget** is an inline HTML+JS block in `index.qmd`. It captures the
visitor's message and sends it to the Worker.

**The Worker** is a tiny JavaScript function running on Cloudflare's global
edge network. It prepends a *system prompt* containing your full background,
then forwards the conversation to OpenAI using your secret API key.

**The key insight** is that the browser never talks to OpenAI directly —
your API key stays server-side, and you can add rate limits or filters later
if you need them.

---

## Part 1 — The Cloudflare Worker

[Wrangler](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/) is the
official Cloudflare CLI. Install it once, authenticate, and you're ready to
deploy:

```bash
npm install -g wrangler
wrangler login   # opens a browser window for OAuth
```

The project needs two files inside a `worker/` directory at your site root.
`wrangler.toml` is the configuration:

```toml
name = "resume-chat"
main = "index.js"
compatibility_date = "2024-09-01"
```

`name` sets the subdomain (`resume-chat.<account>.workers.dev`); `main`
points to the Worker entry-point. That's all the configuration needed.

The real work happens in `index.js`. Scroll through the walkthrough below.

:::{.cr-section}

This is the complete `worker/index.js`. Every line has a job — let's go
through them one by one. @cr-worker

:::{#cr-worker}
```js
const SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are an AI assistant representing Guillaume Gilles.
Answer questions based strictly on the information below.
Be concise, friendly, and professional.
If asked about something not covered, say you don't have that information.

--- PROFILE / EXPERIENCE / EDUCATION / SKILLS ---
…`;

const CORS_HEADERS = {
  "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "https://ggilles.dev",
  "Access-Control-Allow-Methods": "POST, OPTIONS",
  "Access-Control-Allow-Headers": "Content-Type",
};

export default {
  async fetch(request, env) {

    if (request.method === "OPTIONS") {
      return new Response(null, { headers: CORS_HEADERS });
    }

    let body;
    try {
      body = await request.json();
    } catch {
      return new Response("Invalid JSON", { status: 400 });
    }

    const { message, history = [] } = body;

    const messages = [
      { role: "system", content: SYSTEM_PROMPT },
      ...history.slice(-6),
      { role: "user", content: message },
    ];

    const openaiRes = await fetch(
      "https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions", {
        method: "POST",
        headers: {
          "Content-Type": "application/json",
          Authorization: `Bearer ${env.OPENAI_API_KEY}`,
        },
        body: JSON.stringify({
          model: "gpt-4o-mini",
          messages,
          max_tokens: 400,
          temperature: 0.4,
        }),
      }
    );

    const data = await openaiRes.json();
    const reply = data.choices[0].message.content;

    return new Response(JSON.stringify({ reply }), {
      headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json", ...CORS_HEADERS },
    });
  },
};
```
:::

**The system prompt** is the most important part of the setup. It is
prepended to *every* conversation before the visitor's message. The
instruction *"based strictly on the information below"* keeps the model from
hallucinating anything that isn't in your résumé. Keep this section updated
whenever your situation changes. [@cr-worker]{highlight="1-7"}

**CORS headers** tell the browser that your Worker accepts cross-origin
requests. Your widget lives on `ggilles.dev`; the Worker lives on
`workers.dev` — without these headers, the browser would silently block every
fetch. Setting `https://ggilles.dev` restrict access to the worker only from
your exact domain. [@cr-worker]{highlight="9-13"}

**The OPTIONS preflight** is an automatic browser check sent before every
cross-origin POST: *"Are you OK with this?"* The Worker must respond to it
correctly, or the real request never goes through. [@cr-worker]{highlight="18-20"}

**Parsing the body** is straightforward but worth wrapping in a try/catch.
Cloudflare Workers are edge-deployed code — you want clear error responses
rather than uncaught exceptions if something malformed hits the endpoint.
[@cr-worker]{highlight="22-29"}

**Building the messages array** is where context lives. The system prompt
anchors every conversation. `history.slice(-6)` keeps the last three
exchanges (six messages: three user, three assistant) so the model can handle
follow-up questions without ballooning the token count.
[@cr-worker]{highlight="31-35"}

**Calling OpenAI** uses `gpt-4o-mini` — fast, cheap, and well-suited to
short Q&A. `max_tokens: 400` caps reply length so the chat box doesn't
overflow. `temperature: 0.4` keeps answers factual rather than creative.
Crucially, `env.OPENAI_API_KEY` reads from Cloudflare's encrypted secret
store — the key never appears in source code. [@cr-worker]{highlight="37-51"}

**Returning the reply** strips out the OpenAI response envelope and sends
the browser a clean `{ reply: "…" }` JSON object, with the same CORS headers
attached so the browser actually accepts it. [@cr-worker]{highlight="53-58"}

:::

### Storing your key and deploying

Never put secrets in code. Store the OpenAI key in Cloudflare's encrypted
secret store, then deploy:

```bash
cd worker/
wrangler secret put OPENAI_API_KEY   # paste your key when prompted
wrangler deploy
# → Deployed: https://resume-chat.resume-chat.workers.dev
```

Test it immediately from the terminal:

```bash
curl -X POST https://resume-chat.resume-chat.workers.dev \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message": "What is his current job?"}'
# → {"reply":"Guillaume is currently a Regional Economist at the Banque de France…"}
```

---

## Part 2 — The chat widget

The widget is a raw HTML block embedded directly in `index.qmd` using
Quarto's `{=html}` fence. It has two parts: markup and JavaScript.

:::{.cr-section}

The complete widget — HTML structure up top, self-contained JavaScript
below. Let's walk through it. @cr-widget

:::{#cr-widget}
```html
<div id="chat-box">
  <div id="chat-messages">
    <div class="chat-msg bot">
      Hi! I'm an AI assistant. Ask me anything
      about Guillaume's background. 👋
    </div>
  </div>
  <div id="chat-input-row">
    <input id="chat-input" type="text"
           placeholder="e.g. What's his Python experience?"
           autocomplete="off" />
    <button id="chat-send">Send</button>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function () {
  const WORKER_URL = "https://resume-chat.resume-chat.workers.dev";

  const msgs  = document.getElementById("chat-messages");
  const input = document.getElementById("chat-input");
  const send  = document.getElementById("chat-send");
  let history = [];
  let busy    = false;

  send.addEventListener("click", sendMessage);
  input.addEventListener("keydown", (e) => {
    if (e.key === "Enter" && !e.shiftKey) {
      e.preventDefault(); sendMessage();
    }
  });

  function appendMsg(text, role) {
    const div = document.createElement("div");
    div.className = `chat-msg ${role}`;
    div.textContent = text;
    msgs.appendChild(div);
    msgs.scrollTop = msgs.scrollHeight;
    return div;
  }

  async function sendMessage() {
    const text = input.value.trim();
    if (!text || busy) return;
    busy = true; send.disabled = true; input.value = "";

    appendMsg(text, "user");
    const thinking = appendMsg("Thinking…", "bot typing");

    try {
      const res = await fetch(WORKER_URL, {
        method: "POST",
        headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
        body: JSON.stringify({ message: text, history }),
      });
      const data = await res.json();
      const reply = data.reply || data.error || "Something went wrong.";
      thinking.className = "chat-msg bot";
      thinking.textContent = reply;
      history.push({ role: "user",      content: text  });
      history.push({ role: "assistant", content: reply });
    } catch {
      thinking.className = "chat-msg bot";
      thinking.textContent = "⚠️ Could not reach the assistant.";
    }

    busy = false; send.disabled = false;
    input.focus(); msgs.scrollTop = msgs.scrollHeight;
  }
})();
</script>
```
:::

**The HTML structure** is minimal on purpose. `#chat-box` is a flex column:
`#chat-messages` scrolls vertically as the conversation grows; `#chat-input-row`
stays pinned at the bottom. No toggles, no overlays — the box lives inline in
the page, right below the landing page text. [@cr-widget]{highlight="1-14"}

**`WORKER_URL`** is the only value you need to change when you adapt this for
your own site. Everything else is generic. [@cr-widget]{highlight="18"}

**DOM references and state** are grabbed once at startup and kept in
closured variables. `history` accumulates the conversation; `busy` is a
simple mutex that prevents double-sends while a fetch is in flight.
[@cr-widget]{highlight="20-24"}

**Event listeners** wire up two ways to submit: clicking Send and pressing
Enter (but not Shift+Enter, which might be used for line breaks in other
contexts). [@cr-widget]{highlight="26-31"}

**`appendMsg`** is a small factory: it creates a `div`, stamps it with the
right CSS class (`user` or `bot`), appends it to the message area, and
scrolls to the bottom. It returns the element so the caller can mutate it
later (e.g. replace "Thinking…" with the actual reply).
[@cr-widget]{highlight="33-40"}

**`sendMessage`** opens with a guard clause — if the input is empty or a
fetch is already running, it exits immediately. Then it clears the input,
renders the user bubble, and renders a "Thinking…" placeholder that will be
replaced in-place once the response arrives. [@cr-widget]{highlight="42-48"}

**The fetch** posts `{ message, history }` to the Worker. On success, the
placeholder element is updated directly: its class changes from `bot typing`
(italic, dimmed) to `bot`, and its text is replaced with the reply. Both
sides of the exchange are then pushed into `history` for the next round.
[@cr-widget]{highlight="50-61"}

**Error handling and cleanup** cover the case where the Worker is unreachable
(network error, Worker down). After either path, the busy flag is reset, the
Send button re-enabled, and focus returned to the input so the visitor can
keep typing without reaching for the mouse. [@cr-widget]{highlight="62-68"}

:::

### Styles

The widget styles live in `assets/dark.scss` alongside the rest of the site
theme, so they share the same SCSS palette variables and compile into the
same stylesheet:

```scss
$chat-accent:       $phosphor-green;
$chat-accent-hover: darken($phosphor-green, 12%);

#chat-box {
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  border-radius: 0.75rem;
  overflow: hidden;
  border: 1px solid $board-lighter;
  margin-top: 2rem;
}

#chat-messages {
  height: 320px;
  overflow-y: auto;
  padding: 0.75rem;
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  gap: 0.5rem;
  background: $board-dark;
}

.chat-msg {
  max-width: 85%;
  padding: 0.5rem 0.75rem;
  border-radius: 0.6rem;

  &.user  { align-self: flex-end;  background: $chat-accent; color: $board-darkest; }
  &.bot   { align-self: flex-start; background: $board-light; color: $chalk; }
  &.typing { opacity: 0.6; font-style: italic; }
}

#chat-input-row {
  display: flex;
  gap: 0.4rem;
  padding: 0.6rem 0.75rem;
  border-top: 1px solid $board-lighter;
  background: $board-dark;
}

#chat-send {
  background: $chat-accent;
  color: $board-darkest;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 0.4rem;
  padding: 0.4rem 0.75rem;
  cursor: pointer;
  &:hover    { background: $chat-accent-hover; }
  &:disabled { opacity: 0.5; cursor: not-allowed; }
}
```

Because colours are SCSS variables compiled into the theme, the widget
inherits your palette automatically — no JavaScript needed for dark/light
switching.

---

## Part 3 — Integrating with Quarto

Quarto's `{=html}` raw block passes content through to the rendered page
verbatim. Drop the widget anywhere in a `.qmd` file:

````qmd
```{=html}
<div id="chat-box">
  …
</div>
<script>
(function () {
  const WORKER_URL = "https://resume-chat.resume-chat.workers.dev";
  …
})();
</script>
```
````

Because the block is scoped to `index.qmd`, the widget only appears on the
landing page — not on blog posts or teaching pages, where it would be out of
place. If you later want it site-wide, Quarto's `include-after-body` option
in `_quarto.yml` can inject an HTML file into every page instead.

---

## Putting it all together

```bash
# 1. Install Wrangler
npm install -g wrangler

# 2. Authenticate with Cloudflare (creates a free account if needed)
wrangler login

# 3. Deploy the Worker
cd worker/
wrangler deploy
# Note the URL printed: https://resume-chat.<subdomain>.workers.dev

# 4. Store your OpenAI API key
wrangler secret put OPENAI_API_KEY
# Paste your key when prompted

# 5. Update the widget with your Worker URL
# Open index.qmd and change:
#   const WORKER_URL = "https://resume-chat.YOUR-SUBDOMAIN.workers.dev";
# to your actual URL.

# 6. Rebuild the landing page
cd ..
quarto render index.qmd

# 7. Publish
git add -A
git commit -m "Add AI résumé chat assistant"
git push
```

---

## What to customise

**Change the accent colour** — open `assets/dark.scss` and change the
`$chat-accent` variable to any colour in your palette.

**Update the résumé content** — open `worker/index.js` and edit the
`SYSTEM_PROMPT` string. Then run `wrangler deploy` again to push the update.
The website itself does not need to be rebuilt.

**Show the widget on every page** — instead of embedding it in `index.qmd`,
use Quarto's `include-after-body` option in `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
format:
  html:
    include-after-body: _partials/chat-widget.html
```

Move the HTML+JS block to `_partials/chat-widget.html` and `quarto render`
will inject it at the bottom of every page.

**Tighten the CORS policy** — if you only want your own domain to call the
Worker, change the header in `worker/index.js`:

```js
"Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "https://ggilles.dev",
```

**Swap the model** — replace `"gpt-4o-mini"` with `"gpt-4o"` for higher
quality answers (at a higher cost), or try another OpenAI model depending on
your needs.

---

## Closing thoughts

The whole implementation is about 150 lines of code spread across three files
(`worker/index.js`, `assets/dark.scss`, and the raw HTML block in `index.qmd`).
Despite its small footprint, it covers several important concepts that show
up in many real-world projects: serverless functions, CORS, secret management,
prompt engineering, and progressive enhancement (the site works perfectly for
visitors who never open the chat).

If you replicate this on your own site, feel free to open a
[GitHub issue](https://github.com/guillaumegilles/guillaumegilles.github.io)
or reach out on [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/guillaumegilles) — I'd be happy to help you debug it.

```{=html}
<script>
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
  const toc = document.querySelector('#TOC, .sidebar.toc-left, nav[role="doc-toc"]');
  const crSections = document.querySelectorAll('.cr-section');
  if (!toc || !crSections.length) return;
  const observer = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {
    const anyVisible = entries.some(e => e.isIntersecting);
    toc.style.visibility = anyVisible ? 'hidden' : 'visible';
    toc.style.opacity    = anyVisible ? '0'       : '1';
    toc.style.transition = 'opacity 0.2s, visibility 0.2s';
  }, { threshold: 0 });
  crSections.forEach(s => observer.observe(s));
});
</script>
```
